title: Listening - Part 4: Making Products Easier to Use
description: You will hear part of a radio interview with a man called Simon, who is a usability expert. For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

content.audio_script: Presenter: This evening we’re talking to Simon Bell, who advises companies on making everyday products easier to use. Simon, what does usability actually mean? || Simon: People sometimes confuse it with appearance. A beautiful product can still be frustrating. [24]Usability is about whether people can work out what to do quickly and achieve what they want without unnecessary effort[/24]. That can apply to a door handle, a ticket machine or an app. || Presenter: Do ordinary household objects still need improvement? || Simon: Certainly. Take vegetable peelers. The old metal type was effective, but required a fairly particular hand movement. Newer ones have thicker handles and blades that move slightly. [25]For people whose hands are less strong, the modern design is much more comfortable to control[/25]. It’s a small change, but a useful one. || Presenter: Have you ever had trouble with a machine yourself? || Simon: I was once trying to pay for parking in heavy rain. The machine had several screens of instructions. Each screen made sense, but [26]I pressed the wrong button because the steps appeared in an order I didn’t expect[/26]. Good design has to match how people naturally approach a task, especially when they’re in a hurry. || Presenter: What kinds of projects do you work on now? || Simon: I began with kitchen appliances, but I don’t do that any more. These days my work is mostly digital: company websites, online booking systems and mobile applications. [27]I stopped testing household equipment when I joined a firm specialising in online services[/27], although I still notice badly designed kettles in hotels. || Presenter: Why do so many websites annoy users? || Simon: Designers often have too much information and want to display all of it immediately. That may please the organisation, but not the visitor. [28]The usual problem is that nobody has decided clearly what the user needs to do first[/28]. A page becomes crowded because the planning stage was weak, not because the technology is difficult. || Presenter: Are companies more aware of this than before? || Simon: Much more. Ten years ago, I had to explain why testing with real customers was worth paying for. Now, [29]companies recognise that people who cannot complete a purchase online will simply buy from a competitor[/29]. Keeping customers satisfied is no longer an optional extra; it directly affects income. || Presenter: Is there one particular route into usability work? || Simon: Not at all. We employ computer graduates, of course, but also people from psychology, graphic design and customer service. [30]Many of the best new staff have already spent years in other jobs and understand how real customers behave[/30]. Technical skills can be learned; curiosity about people is essential.

task.title: Part 4
task.instruction: For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

questions.question: How does Simon explain the meaning of usability?

options.A: It is mainly concerned with how attractive products appear.
options.B: It involves making tasks simple for the people doing them.
options.C: It compares objects designed for different purposes.

questions.explanation: Simon says usability means helping people achieve what they want quickly and without unnecessary effort.

questions.question: What does Simon say about modern vegetable peelers?

options.A: They are easier for some users to handle.
options.B: They produce better results than old models.
options.C: They are more difficult to clean.

questions.explanation: He explains that people with less strength in their hands can control modern designs more comfortably.

questions.question: What problem did Simon have with the parking machine?

options.A: Its instructions were impossible to read in the rain.
options.B: It asked him for too much information.
options.C: The sequence of actions was confusing.

questions.explanation: Simon pressed the wrong button because the steps appeared in an unexpected order.

questions.question: Which area of usability is Simon no longer involved in?
questions.explanation: Simon says that he stopped testing household equipment when he began specialising in online services.

questions.question: What does Simon think is the main cause of poor website design?

options.A: Visitors expect to find too much information.
options.B: The site's main purpose has not been planned clearly.
options.C: The available technology is not reliable enough.

questions.explanation: He says crowded sites are usually caused by weak planning about what the user should do first.

questions.question: What does Simon say about most companies now?

options.A: They understand that usability affects sales.
options.B: They employ customers to design their websites.
options.C: They prefer to test products after they are launched.

questions.explanation: Companies know users will buy elsewhere if they cannot complete a purchase online.

questions.question: According to Simon, who can be especially successful in usability work?

options.A: people who have worked in a range of other fields
options.C: designers who avoid contact with customers

questions.explanation: Simon values staff with experience in other jobs because they understand real customers.
